sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Vulvar cancer most commonly presents in older individuals, with a median age of diagnosis of 68 years. The majority of vulvar cancers are squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), accounting for approximately 90% of cases. The majority of vulvar cancers are located in the labia majora, though other possible sites include the labia minora, clitoris, mons pubis, and perineum [MS-2]. Patients with HPV-negative tumors often present with a single mass or ulcer on the labia majora or minora, while HPV-positive tumors are more frequently multifocal and may be associated with concurrent cervical neoplasia [MS-4]. Pruritus and pain/irritation are common presenting symptoms; vulvar bleeding or discharge may also occur. A majority of patients present with early-stage localized disease. Although many cases may be asymptomatic, symptoms often prompt evaluation leading to diagnosis. Rarer histologies include melanoma, extramammary Paget disease, Bartholin gland adenocarcinoma, verrucous carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and sarcoma [MS-2].

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Itching of the vulvar skin, a common presenting symptom that may be persistent and progressive.

symptoms.name: Pain or irritation
symptoms.detail: Discomfort in the vulvar region, which may be constant or intermittent.

symptoms.name: Vulvar bleeding
symptoms.detail: Spontaneous bleeding from the vulvar lesion or bleeding associated with trauma to the area.

symptoms.name: Vulvar discharge
symptoms.detail: Abnormal discharge from a vulvar lesion, which may be serous, bloody, or purulent.

symptoms.name: Palpable mass
symptoms.detail: A mass or nodule on the vulva that may be self-detected or found during clinical examination.

symptoms.name: Ulceration
symptoms.detail: A non-healing ulcer on the vulvar skin, which may be painless in early stages.

symptoms.name: Vulvar dystrophy symptoms
symptoms.detail: Changes in vulvar skin color, texture, or thickness that may precede or accompany malignancy.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Spontaneous bleeding from the vulvar lesion or bleeding associated with trauma to the area.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: A mass or nodule on the vulva that may be self-detected or found during clinical examination.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: A non-healing ulcer on the vulvar skin, which may be painless in early stages.
